October is typically the driest and hottest month in Cayenne, French Guiana (closest to the Equator), with daily average temperatures around 27.5°C. January, conversely, lies in the wet season, with heavy precipitation, extensive cloud cover, and lower daily averages around 26.1°C. Given a difference in monthly means of ~1.4°C and an average daily standard deviation around 0.8°C, the probability that a day in January is hotter than a day in October is essentially the chance of anomalous rainy weather suppressing temperatures on the October date while sunny weather elevates them on the January date. Statistically, this represents roughly a 10-15% chance.

Commercial Real Estate (CRE) loans made by commercial banks have seen stalling growth recently due to higher interest rates, stricter lending standards, and concerns over the office space sector. Banks represent a significant share of CRE lending, but face a massive maturity wall in 2024-2025. Given the trend of banks trying to reduce CRE exposure and offloading loans to private credit, there is a substantial chance that nominal CRE balances at commercial banks could decrease or remain flat. However, since non-office CRE (like industrial and multifamily) is fundamentally stronger and general inflation pushes loan sizes up, nominal balances may continue to see slow growth. Given the competing pressures of loan write-offs versus inflation-driven growth in non-office sectors, the likelihood leans slightly towards an increase over a multi-year horizon, but risk of a minor contraction is significant. Therefore, I assign a 55.2% probability that it will be higher. *0.552*

The Treasury typically targets a lower TGA at the end of December (targeted $700B for 2024) compared to late October (~$800B). Furthermore, the debt limit suspension ends on January 1, 2025. After January 1, the Treasury will be unable to issue net new debt and will deploy extraordinary measures, drawing down the TGA to fund the government. Net cash flows in January typically do not support a massive increase in TGA without debt issuance. Thus, the TGA in late January 2025 should be significantly lower than its late October 2024 levels, making an increase highly unlikely.
